findings.title: zapOut uses the requested withdrawal amount instead of the amount actually delivered by the batcher

best_agent_match.title: zapOut trusts the nominal withdrawal amount instead of measuring what the batcher actually delivered

rejected_candidates.title: Missing slippage protection for batcher withdrawal in zapOut
rejected_candidates.reason: Final-output slippage is already user-bounded by `minAmountOut`; the reportable edge case is the separate stale-balance subsidy issue captured in F-005, not a generic lack of slippage protection.

rejected_candidates.title: No zero-address validation for governance in sweep
rejected_candidates.reason: This only turns a governance misconfiguration into governance self-harm; it is not a permissionless or protocol-level exploit in the zapper itself.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing event emissions for governance operations
rejected_candidates.reason: Observability issue only; no realistic fund-loss, theft, lockup, or DoS impact.

rejected_candidates.title: Unchecked call return values in zap function
rejected_candidates.reason: The low-level call success flag is checked, and swap success is further bounded by balance-delta plus `minAmountOut`; the meaningful risk from a no-op target is the persistent arbitrary approval issue already captured in F-001/F-003.

rejected_candidates.title: Floating pragma allows incompatible compiler versions
rejected_candidates.reason: Informational build-hygiene concern, not a concrete protocol-level vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: No access control on critical functions
rejected_candidates.reason: `sweep()` is explicitly restricted to `vault.governance()`; a compromised governance key is an out-of-scope trust assumption, not a missing access control bug.
